name: Buddhist Mythology
description: An enlightened being who delays entry into final nirvana in order to help all sentient beings achieve liberation. The bodhisattva ideal is central to Mahayana Buddhism. Major bodhisattvas include Avalokiteshvara (compassion) and Manjushri (wisdom).
definition: An enlightened being in Mahayana Buddhism who postpones final Nirvana to help all sentient beings achieve liberation, embodying the ideal of compassionate self-sacrifice and unlimited service.
practitioners: All followers of the Bodhisattva path
applications: Meditation on universal suffering and its cessation
Transformation of enlightenment for communal benefit

keyMyths.title: The Vessantara Jataka
keyMyths.description: Prince Vessantara embodies perfect generosity, giving away his kingdom's prized elephant, his horses, chariot, and even his children and wife, testing the limits of selflessness and demonstrating the path to enlightenment through extreme renunciation. This Jataka tale highlights the bodhisattva's unwavering commitment to helping others, even at great personal cost.

keyMyths.title: Avalokiteshvara's Thousand Arms
keyMyths.description: Seeing the suffering of the world, Avalokiteshvara vowed to help all beings. His head split into eleven pieces, and his arms multiplied to a thousand, each with an eye, symbolizing his vast compassion and ability to perceive and alleviate suffering across the universe. This myth illustrates the boundless nature of the bodhisattva's compassion.

keyMyths.title: Manjushri's Sword of Wisdom
keyMyths.description: Manjushri, the bodhisattva of wisdom, wields a flaming sword that cuts through ignorance and delusion. He is often depicted riding a lion, symbolizing the power of wisdom to overcome obstacles. This myth showcases the importance of wisdom in achieving enlightenment and guiding others on the path.

keyMyths.title: The Bodhisattva's Descent from Tushita Heaven
keyMyths.description: Before becoming the Buddha, Siddhartha Gautama resided in Tushita Heaven as a bodhisattva. He chose to be reborn into the human realm at the most opportune time to teach the Dharma and liberate beings from suffering. This narrative explains the bodhisattva's deliberate choice to engage with the world's suffering.

keyMyths.title: Tara's Origin from Avalokiteshvara's Tears
keyMyths.description: According to legend, Tara was born from a tear of Avalokiteshvara, shed out of compassion for the suffering of sentient beings. She embodies swift compassion and active intervention, swiftly answering the prayers of those in need. This myth highlights the active and compassionate nature of the bodhisattva path.

sources.name: Jataka-Mala
sources.description: A collection of Jataka tales, stories of the Buddha's past lives, illustrating the development of his bodhisattva qualities.
sources.title: Jataka-Mala

sources.name: Karandavyuha Sutra
sources.description: A Mahayana sutra that describes the origins and powers of Avalokiteshvara, the bodhisattva of compassion.
sources.title: Karandavyuha Sutra

sources.name: Manjushri Namasamgiti
sources.description: A tantric text dedicated to Manjushri, the bodhisattva of wisdom.
sources.title: Manjushri Namasamgiti

sources.name: Lalitavistara Sutra
sources.description: A Mahayana sutra recounting the life of the Buddha, from his descent from Tushita Heaven to his enlightenment.
sources.title: Lalitavistara Sutra

sources.name: Tara Tantras
sources.description: A collection of tantric texts dedicated to the goddess Tara, emphasizing her swift action and compassion.
sources.title: Tara Tantras
